attributes.stem:
A 47-year-old pre-menopausal woman presents to her general practitioner complaining of urinary incontinence. This typically occurs when laughing or sneezing and she must wear a pad to prevent leaking when doing weights at the gym. Her medical history includes three deliveries. She is currently visiting a physiotherapist who is helping her out with pelvic floor exercises.

The only abnormal finding on physical examination is a small uterine prolapse, with the cervix dropping into the vagina.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate step in her management?
attributes.explanation:
Urinary incontinence is a common cause for presentation to general practice. The stem describes stress incontinence (as opposed to urge incontinence), which occurs secondary to pelvic floor and/or urethral sphincter weakness and is characteristically triggered by activities that raise intra-abdominal pressure. Whilst usually something that is thought to be more characteristically seen in post-menopausal women, it is important to note that incontinence is a presentation that can also commonly occur in the pre-menopausal demographic. Risk factors include multiple pregnancies, vaginal delivery, menopause,, smoking and chronic cough. All of management options shouldbe accompanied by lifestyle changes to address modifiable risk factors.

The best initial management for stress incontinence is pelvic floor training. This should be guided by a physiotherapist or continence nurse in order to ensure the greatest chance of success. A trial of three months is typically recommended. If this patient was post-menopausal, she would probably also be given oestrogen cream as an adjunct to pelvic floor exercises.

A vaginal pessary is a removable device that is placed in the vagina to compress the urethra against pubic bone and lift the neck of the bladder so that urine does not leak. A pessary is administered in first degree uterine prolapse along with pelvic floor training. This patient has a stage 1 uterine prolapse.

A bladder neck support prosthesis is a non-surgical option that essentially acts as a pessary for bladder support without prolapse. This has largely fallen out of favour since the introduction of minimally invasive surgical options.

Bladder re-training, such as timed voiding, is used in the treatment of urge incontinence.

There are no pharmacological options for the treatment of stress incontinence. Oxybutynin is an anticholinergic agent which acts by reducing parasympathetic response and subsequently detrusor activity. It is therefore only used if there is an element of urge incontinence.

Tension-free vaginal tape (TVT) is perhaps the most effective treatment for stress incontinence with a success rate around 95%. The operation involves insertion of TVT behind the urethra via a small vaginal incision. However, simpler measures should be tried first.
learningPoints: Pelvic floor exercises are an effective and non-invasive treatment for stress incontinence in women.
Vaginal pessaries can be used to manage stress incontinence associated with uterine prolapse.
Stress urinary incontinence is often triggered by activities that increase intra-abdominal pressure, such as laughing or sneezing.
references: Nilsson CG, Kuuva N, Falconer C, Rezapour M, Ulmsten U. Long-term results of the tension-free vaginal tape (TVT) procedure for surgical treatment of female stress urinary incontinence. Int Urogynecol J Pelvic Floor Dysfunct. 2001;12 Suppl 2:S5-8. doi: 10.1007/s001920170003. PMID: 11450979. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11450979/

The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners. Guidelines for preventive activities in general practice, 9th edition. East Melbourne: RACGP; 2018. [Internet]. Accessed 12 Jan 2025. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/FSDEDEV/media/documents/Clinical%20Resources/Guidelines/Red%20Book/Guidelines-for-preventive-activities-in-general-practice.pdf

Ontario Health (Quality). Vaginal Pessaries for Pelvic Organ Prolapse or Stress Urinary Incontinence: A Health Technology Assessment. Ont Health Technol Assess Ser. 2021 May 6;21(3):1-155. PMID: 34055111; PMCID: PMC8129636. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34055111/

Al-Shaikh G, Syed S, Osman S, Bogis A, Al-Badr A. Pessary use in stress urinary incontinence: a review of advantages, complications, patient satisfaction, and quality of life. Int J Womens Health. 2018 Apr 17;10:195-201. doi: 10.2147/IJWH.S152616. PMID: 29713205; PMCID: PMC5909791. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29713205/
